### Post by 0x845fed on 2008-12-29
Hello.  I'm having some problems configuring my GRUB boot loader to get me into Ubuntu.  

IDE hard disk with Solaris
SATA disk with two partitions:
[LIST=1]
[*]Ubuntu
[*]CentOS
[/LIST]
IDE slave, which is empty

I get the following error when trying to boot Ubuntu:
```
Grub Error 13: "Invalid or unsupported executable format"
```

Here is my /boot/grub/menu.lst file:
```
# grub.conf generated by anaconda
#
# Note that you do not have to rerun grub after making changes to this file
# NOTICE:  You do not have a /boot partition.  This means that
#          all kernel and initrd paths are relative to /, eg.
#          root (hd2,2)
#          kernel /boot/vmlinuz-version ro root=/dev/sda3
#          initrd /boot/initrd-version.img
#boot=/dev/hda
default=1
timeout=60
splashimage=(hd2,2)/boot/grub/splash.xpm.gz
hiddenmenu
title CentOS
        root (hd2,2)
        kernel /boot/xen.gz-2.6.18-92.el5
        module /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.18-92.el5xen ro root=LABEL=/ rhgb quiet
        module /boot/initrd-2.6.18-92.el5xen.img
title Solaris
        rootnoverify (hd0,0)
        chainloader +1
title Ubuntu
        rootnoverify (hd2,0)
        chainloader +1

```

Also...

```

# fdisk -l
Disk /dev/hda: 300.0 GB, 300090728448 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 36483 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/hda1   *           2       36483   293041665   bf  Solaris

Disk /dev/hdd: 20.0 GB, 20020396032 bytes
16 heads, 63 sectors/track, 38792 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 1008 * 512 = 516096 bytes

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System

Disk /dev/sda: 1000.2 GB, 1000204886016 bytes
255 heads, 63 sectors/track, 121601 cylinders
Units = cylinders of 16065 * 512 = 8225280 bytes

   Device Boot      Start         End      Blocks   Id  System
/dev/sda1               1       23829   191406411   83  Linux
/dev/sda2           23830       24315     3903795    5  Extended
/dev/sda3   *       24316       49500   202298512+  83  Linux
/dev/sda5           23830       24315     3903763+  82  Linux swap / Solaris

```

I can boot into Solaris and CentOS just fine.  Can someone point me in the right direction?

Thanks in advance.

### Post by kokkus on 2008-12-29
All you gotta do is to reset the grub menu.
Follow this guide:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=224351&highlight=reset+grub](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=224351&highlight=reset+grub)

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-12-29
How about trying:
```
sudo grub
grub> device (hd0) /dev/sda
grub> root (hd0,0)
grub> setup (hd0,0)
grub> quit
```
Reboot, and let me know what happens when you try and boot Ubuntu again.

---

### Post by 0x845fed on 2008-12-30
Cool, that worked just fine.

Can you help me understand what happened?  What I gather is that when I installed Ubuntu it put GRUB on.  But when I put CentOS on, it overwrote Ubuntu's GRUB.  I just had to reinstall GRUB at the beginning of the Ubuntu partition.  Am I close?

---

### Post by caljohnsmith on 2008-12-30
> **0x845fed said:**
> Cool, that worked just fine.

Can you help me understand what happened?  What I gather is that when I installed Ubuntu it put GRUB on.  But when I put CentOS on, it overwrote Ubuntu's GRUB.  I just had to reinstall GRUB at the beginning of the Ubuntu partition.  Am I close?
You're right, when you installed CentOS, it by default installed Grub to the MBR (Master Boot Record) of the HDD and took over the booting process. I noticed that the CentOS Grub entry for Ubuntu uses "chainloader", which means that Grub will try and boot the boot sector of that partition (the first sector of the partition), which is the way Windows normally boots for example. But to make that work, you have to have a boot loader installed to the partition's boot sector, so those commands I gave you installed Grub to the boot sector of the sda1 Ubuntu partition. As an alternative, you could instead change your CentOS menu.lst to use the following entry for Ubuntu:
```
title Ubuntu Grub Menu
root (hd0,0)
configfile /boot/grub/menu.lst
```
That way the Ubuntu's menu.lst gets loaded without going through the intermediate step of booting the Ubuntu partition boot sector. Anyway, cheers and have fun with all your OSes. :)

---
